course_title: The Fundamentals of Designing and Staffing Organisations
course_description: The aim of this course is to provide students with the fundamental mindset, critical thinking skills, and competencies associated with positive performance enhancing staffing practices and work design in a complex, dynamic world. This course provides essential learning for line managers, those who wish to pursue a career in Human Resource Management, and anyone who wants to know how to make their organisation world class in talent management and workplace integration and engagement.

requirements.assumed_background: No background in Human Resources Management or previous work experience is assumed, although if you bring this background to the course you will be positioned to make the most of both your learning and a contribution to the learning of your classmates. You will be encouraged to engage with family, friends and other colleagues to learn from their experiences at work in order to make practical sense of the material covered in this course.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
The dates in the Learning Activities section are the Monday of each week. Classes are currently scheduled on both Wednesdays and Fridays. The activities listed will be presented for at each class on the relevant day.
Please attend the scheduled class as per your enrolment to ensure manageable class sizes and therefore quality attention to your learning.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of this course is to provide students with an understanding of how organisations are designed and staffed, practical skills in the key staffing processes and an appreciation for how effective staffing helps organisations meet business objectives. The latest research in business ethics and positive and strengths-based approaches to Strategic HRM underpins the course.

learning_outcomes.description: Explain how organisational design and strategic staffing aid businesses to meet their objectives.
Design essential aids to the staffing process, incorporating both team and role design, recruitment and selection tools, while also ensuring the legal requirements for these processes are addressed.

Critically evaluate the appropriateness of different organisational design, recruitment and selection processes for different role types and circumstances, within an organisational context and considering multiple stakeholders.
Elaborate on how organisational design and staffing practices that take ethics, diversity and employee wellbeing into account add value to organisations and society.
Present evidence-based arguments and well-designed recommendations to senior stakeholders for aspects of organisational design and staffing practices.

assessment_details.title: Contemporary Practice in Org Design and Staffing
assessment_details.due_date: Design Brief:2pm,day B4 seminar,Poster:2pm, day B4 presentation, Team Assessment: 1 wk later
assessment_details.task_description:
As a team of six (6) people you will choose or be allocated a specific seminar week (eg. Seminar 3) in Week 1. Your team’s task is to design and present a poster that addresses either a " latest thinking " OR “ leading practice ” aspect of your chosen seminar week’ s content (ie for Seminar 3). The overall aim of this assessment is for you to further develop your teamwork skills and, working with your team members identify, research and present on a contemporary topic that specifically interests your team and will be illuminating and engaging for your colleagues. Your poster should NOT replicate the seminar material that I will present but add to it with a contemporary focus.

Teams will be formed in Week 1 and I will set up a group for you on Blackboard to support your collaboration and allow submission of your assessment items. If there are any changes in the membership of your team you must advise me immediately.

If your team choses the “ latest thinking ” approach, your poster topic will mainly focus on insights, trends or practices published in the academic or practitioner press in the last two (2) years. If you chose a “ leading practice ” approach, your poster topic will mainly focus on the recent (within last 2 years) application of a contemporary practice in a large organisation recognised for excellence in this practice.

In order to develop your ideas, your team must read and discuss the required chapters and papers for your seminar week and then identify a specific area of interest for further research. From this further research, you will formulate your specific poster topic . In addition, you must identify a business responsibility perspective on your poster topic and address a relevant ethical, legal, diversity OR employee wellbeing aspect of your chosen topic in your poster.

This assessment requires you, with your team members, to complete the following three (3) items:

1. Poster Design Brief (5% - team)

The aim of this part of the assessment is for your team to identify and describe a viable poster topic to be approved by me.

Each team member must read one good quality paper relevant to your assigned week of content and share the points of interest with the team. Within your team, each person must read something different and you must ensure that all of the required papers for your week of content are covered. You do not need to read and summarise the text book chapter. You can add a peer reviewed paper from one of the journals listed in the Learning Resources section of Blackboard that is relevant to your poster topic or a contemporary practitioner report or article from a well-respected source.

Your team must submit a two (2) page design brief that includes:

Your team name and allocated seminar week.

Your proposed topic as a brief statement or question.

The 3-4 key ideas or points you want to explore within that topic.

Any case studies or examples you intend to include (1-2 sentences)

Your current idea of an business responsibility perspective, encompassing legal, ethical, diversity OR employee wellbeing issues, relevant to your topic. (1-2 sentences)

The 3-4 key academic or practitioner papers you intend to draw on for your poster. You must provide a full reference for each of them in the Academy of Management Journal (AMJ) style.

Your team members names, student numbers and their roles as team leader, researcher(s), presenter (s), poster production and poster creativity or any other roles that may be assigned.

An interim team work reflection on a) what is working well, and areas to improve in your team work. (using the Teamwork Peer Assessment criteria, see details on BB)

As a single appendix , each team member must provide 500 words (max) summarising the key points from the paper that they reviewed. This must include:-

Their name and student number

The full reference to the paper in the correct AMJ referencing style;

The type of paper – refer to the “Academic and Practitioner Sources” guide covered in Seminar 1.

4-5 bullet points summarising key ideas from the paper that are relevant to your chosen topic

The design brief must be submitted through the Assessments folder by 2:00 pm on the day prior to your assigned seminar week . In the seminar, I may call on you to briefly comment on your papers at a relevant point.

To further help you have a clear and well understood foundation for your poster, you must arrange for representatives of your team (or the whole team) to meet with myself either immediately after class in your seminar week or within the next 24 hrs for a 15 minute review of your design brief and discuss and confirm your topic. Please arrange a mutually convenient time with me by e-mail ( t.martin@business.uq.edu.au ) at least 1 week prior.

IMPORTANT: I need to discuss your choice of poster topic with your BEFORE you progress with the assignment.

A brief rubric for this assessment is available on BB, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Demonstrated understanding of your topic

Inclusion of all required material in the design brief

Quality of proposed references

Accuracy of referencing style

Preparation and attendance at the Design Brief review.

2. Poster (20% - team)

Based on your design brief , your team will prepare and present an A1 sized poster on your approved topic.

The aim of this part of the assessment is for your team to engage your colleagues through an in-depth look at a specific practice or case that is contemporary, interesting and not otherwise covered in the seminar materials. A more detailed guideline and hints and tips for poster creation will be available in the Assessments folder on BlackBoard. One very important tip is that you should ensure that the minimum font size is 24 pt, except for footnotes, minor annotations and your reference list...otherwise your classmates will not be able to read your poster!

Your team will present your poster to your peers at one of the four (4) Poster Presentation Sessions scheduled throughout the semester.

Posters for Seminar 2, 3 and 4 will be presented in Week 5

Posters for Seminar 5, 6, and 7 will be presented in Week 8

Posters for Seminar 8, 9 and 10 will be presented in Week 11

Posters for Seminars 11 and 12 will be presented in Week 13 and all other posters displayed as part of the course review.

A PDF of your poster must be submitted through the Assessments folder by 2:00 pm on the day prior to your Poster Presentation Sessions. You must bring a printed version of your poster to class for your Poster Presentation Session.

During the Poster Presentation Session each team will be given eight (8) minutes to present their poster and two (2) minutes to respond questions from the audience. One, several or all members of the team may be involved in the presentation. The audience will provide feedback to each team and also vote on the best poster overall for that Presentation Session .

The full rubric for this assessment is available on BB, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Topic focus on "latest thinking" or "leading practice"

Discussion of relevant legal, ethical, diversity or employee wellbeing perspective

Poster appearance

Presentation style

3. Teamwork Peer Assessment (5% - individual)

Following your time working together, you will evaluate the contribution of each of your team members to the team project. The aim of this part of the assessment is to highlight the importance of working effectively in a team. Your individual mark will be the average of your team members assessment of your input.

You will individually complete this assessment for your colleagues no later than 1 week after your team has presented your poster.

In summary:

As an example, if your seminar week is Week 3 , and you attend the Thursday class, your team must:-

E-mail me no later than Thursday, Week 2 to arrange a 15 min meeting with me for the following week.

Submit your design brief on Wednesday, Week 3 , 2:00 pm.

Meet with me either after the Thursday, Week 3 seminar finishes or on the Friday.

Submit your poster on Wednesday, Week 5 , 2:00 pm.

Present your poster at Thursday, Week 5 seminar.

Individually submit your team assessment no later than Thursday, Week 6 .

Bring your poster to the Week 13 seminar to display near your group’s table.

Use this example to work out your own key dates, depending on your chosen seminar week.

assessment_details.title: Recruitment & Selection Strategy
assessment_details.due_date: Position Desc: 2pm, Mon 19 Aug; R&S Strategy Preso: 2pm day b4 Wk 7 seminar, Report: 2pm, Wed 9 Oct
assessment_details.task_description:
INDIVIDUALLY, you will select a job that interests you and develop a detailed recruitment and selection strategy to fill a vacancy in this role. The purpose of this assessment is to give you hands-on experience in the design of a recruitment and selection strategy and further develop your professional and business writing skills by you preparing a detailed, evidence based recommendation for a senior stakeholder. The Portfolio will comprise three parts, as detailed below.

1. Position Description (10%)

The aim of this part of the assessment is for you to demonstrate that you have mastered the practical skills of preparing a comprehensive position description to later support the development of a recruitment and selection strategy.

INDIVUALLY choose a job that you are interested in and where you are able to speak to one or more job incumbents in order to understand the job requirements in detail.

As soon as possible , but no later than 2:00 pm Monday 5 August , send an e-mail to me ( t.martin@business.uq.edu.au ), detailing the job title, the organisation, the job location and a 1-2 sentences about the main purpose of the job. You must also confirm your access to job incumbents and/or the job manager so that you can discuss the details of the job when you prepare the Position Description.

IMPORTANT: I need to approve your choice of job BEFORE you progress with the assignment.

You will interview your contact for the job and conduct a job analysis. From this you will prepare a detailed Position Description drawing on the tools and methods we discuss in Seminar 2 . You will need to clearly identify the business context for the job, the job tasks and responsibilities and the knowledge, skills, abilities and other characteristics (KSAO’s) that a person will require to be successful in the job. You will also need to evaluate the design of the job using the models covered in Seminar 2.

Submit your Position Description with a brief covering note via Turnitin in the Assessments folder by Monday, 19th August , 2:00 pm . Please include PD_ and the title of your job in the name of your file. For example: PD_Mecca_Cosmetics_Saleperson.pdf.

The full rubric for this assessment is available on BB, but in summary the criteria for this assessment address:

Comprehensive description of job context

Detailed description of the job responsibilities and task

Comprehensive description of the related person specification (KSAO’s)

Thoughtful evaluation of the job design

Professional and practical presentation

Your marks and feedback will be returned by Monday 26th August so that you can continue to develop your portfolio.

2. Recruitment and Selection Strategy Presentation (10%)

The aim of this part of the assessment is for you to outline your approach to fill a vacancy in your selected role and for you to get feedback from your peers and from the marking team on your intended approach. This early input will help you to prepare the third part of your Portfolio, your detailed Recruitment and Selection Strategy Recommendation Report .

Working from your position description, and in the context of the organisation you have selected, you will design a recruitment and selection strategy to fill a single vacancy in your job and present this to your colleagues in a small group during the Week 7 seminar.

If you wish to recruit more than one person for the role, you must get approval from the Course Co-ordinator (e-Mail: t.martin@business.uq.edu.au ) by Week 6 at the latest.

You will need to set the context for your colleagues by describing the role context and the business objectives for filling the role.

Your recruitment strategy should include your Position Description , revised if needed, and sections detailing your choices of the most effective methods to attract applicants from relevant target markets including consideration of relevant industry analysis and labour market conditions, identification of candidate sources, and a targeted advertising strategy with media choices and advertising copy appropriate for at least one of your media choices.

Your selection strategy should address your choice of selection criteria in line with the business context and then outline the selection process, matching the essential and any desirable selection criteria with your choice of the most effective selection tools to evaluate those criteria and select a candidate to appoint to your role.

In addition, your presentation should briefly discuss the volume of applicants you expect, your decision making approach and the cost and practicality of your recruitment and selection approach.

You will document your strategy in a brief Word document or PowerPoint presentation with additional notes, drawing on a template provided in the Portfolio - Recruitment & Selection Strategy folder on Blackboard. You do not need to follow this template exactly and feel free to be creative, as long as you provide the required information. If you use a PowerPoint presentation, you can add explanatory material in the notes section and submit a PDF of the “Notes Page” format.

Submit your R&S Strategy presentation via Turnitin in Assessments> Portfolio - Recruitment & Selection Strategy folder by 2:00 pm on the day prior to your seminar in Week 7 . Please include STRATEGY_ and the title of your job in the name of your file. For example: STRATEGY_Mecca_Cosmetics_Saleperson.pdf.

In the Week 7 seminar, you will be randomly assigned to small groups for presentations and peer feedback. You will provide one (1) hard copy of your presentation for your designated Peer Reviewer in your group. You will give a five (5) minute presentation of your strategy to your peers with a further two (2) minutes allowed for questions and feedback. At the end, your assigned Peer Reviewer in your group will provide you with brief written feedback and you will prepare some feedback for another colleague in the group.

The full rubric for this assessment is available on BB, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Comprehensive yet concise outline of the role context and business objectives

Comprehensive Position Description

Clearly targeted and practical recruitment strategy

Valid, reliable and practical selection strategy

Effective (draft) job advertisement

Effective oral and written presentation

Your marks and feedback will be returned by Wednesday 18th September so that you can continue to develop your portfolio.

3. Recruitment and Selection Strategy Report (30%)

The aim of this part of the assessment is for you to apply and integrate the theories, models and concepts from this course by articulating the evidence-base to support your recruitment and selection strategy, considering implementation issues and to further develop your business writing skills by presenting your recommendation to a senior stakeholder.

In this assignment, you will take on the role of an HR consultant in a consulting business. You will present a detailed and evidenced based recommendation to me, as the Principal Consultant, and your manager, for the recruitment and selection strategy you have previously presented to your colleagues. Not every manager would expect this level of justification, but I place particular importance on the application of evidence based practice.

You will be seeking approval to initiate the recruitment and selection strategy as you have designed it on behalf of your “client” organisation. Your recommendation will be structured as a report, and will include a critical analysis drawing on the academic literature to explain and justify your choices of tools to be used in recruitment and selection and the overall process to come to a final decision about the successful applicant. Your Position Description , revised using all previous feedback, should be included as an appendix.

Clear and concise written communication is an important skill to develop and hone. A template will be provided with guidance on the required sections. The word limit is 2000 words (excluding references, appendices, up to three tables with minimal text and the executive summary). Text beyond 2000 words will be neither read nor assessed. Appendices must be referenced in-text, presented in order of that reference, and clearly necessary for you to present your recommendation.

In order to argue for your strategy with evidence from current research on recruitment and selection, you must use a minimum of ten (10) good quality, peer reviewed journal articles to support your arguments. Please, please, pretty please use the reading list provided as a starting point. You may also draw on other good quality sources as appropriate but avoid references to text books . All sources must be cited correctly and included in a reference list according to the Academy of Management Journal (AMJ) referencing style.

Please note that aside from the quality of the references, what is most important is how you use the evidence from these sources to justify your recruitment and selection strategy. This involves comparing and contrasting alternate perspectives and choosing options that most suit the job and the organisation that you have chosen.

Submit your report via Turnitin in the in Assessments> Portfolio - Recruitment & Selection Strategy folder by Friday, 24th May, 2:00 pm . Please include REPORT_ and the title of your job in the name of your file. For example: REPORT_Mecca_Cosmetics_Saleperson.pdf.

I highly recommend that you submit a final draft and review your TurnitIn report at least 24 hours before the assignment due date to ensure that you have not inadvertently failed to comply with the academic integrity policy. Please check your citations, quotations (these should be minimal) and references carefully.

The full rubric for this assessment is available on BB, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Complete Executive Summary

Comprehensive yet concise outline of the business context

Clearly targeted, evidence-based recruitment strategy

Valid, reliable and evidence-based selection strategy

Practical implementation approach

Effective use of literature

Well-structured and written business report

In summary:

In order to complete this portfolio, you must

E-mail me no later than 2:00 pm Monday 5 August with the details of your job for approval.

Submit your Position Description with a brief covering note by 2:00 pm Monday, 19th August .

Marks will be returned by Monday, 26th August .

Submit your R&S Strategy presentation by 2:00 pm on the day prior to your seminar in Week 7 .

Marks will be returned by Wednesday, 18th September .

Submit your Report by 2:00 pm Wed, 9th October .

assessment_details.title: Organisational Design
assessment_details.task_description:
INDIVIDUALLY, you will write a brief analysis in response to a case that has been previously discussed in our seminars. The purpose of this assessment is for you to engage with the organisational design models and staffing frameworks discussed, apply them in the context of business growth and to encourage reflection on your learning journey.

In this assignment, you will take on the role of an HR consultant reporting to me, as the Principal Consultant of our MGTS7612 consulting business. The case will be introduced in Week 11 and discussed in subsequent weeks. The CEO of the case business will be seeking our advice on a number of questions. Your understanding and appreciation for the case will be greatly assisted if you attend and actively participate in the seminars each week. In Week 13, the specific case scenario for this assignment will be provided. Your task will be to identify and discuss organisational design issues likely to arise in the creation of a team to explore a new product offering for the case organisation. In addition, you will be asked to reflect on what you have learned over the semester.

Your analysis will address the three (3) questions raised in the case scenario and will be a minimum of 1000 words, maximum 1500 words (excluding references). You will be writing for a business audience but will include citations from relevant sources to support your analysis. You must use a minimum of three (3) good quality sources. Please use the course reading list provided as a starting point but avoid references to text books. All sources must be cited correctly and included in a reference list according to the Academy of Management Journal (AMJ) referencing style.

I highly recommend that you submit a final draft and review your TurnitIn report at least 24 hours before the assignment due date to ensure that you have not inadvertently failed to comply with the academic integrity policy. Please check your citations, quotations (these should be minimal) and references carefully.

The full rubric for this assessment is available on BB, but in summary the criteria for this assessment are:

Relevant issues identified and discussed.

Excellent understanding of staffing or organisational design frameworks applied

Thoughtful reflection on learning

Relevant literature used.

Business writing style

learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/MGTS7612 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Text Book

For many of you, this is your first exposure to the field of Human Resource Management. In order to help you understand the background for this course, I now reference a recent HRM textbook, Stone, R. J. (2017). Human Resource Management (9th ed.). Milton, Qld: Wiley. I will refer to approx. 40% of this book and it will be an important source of background information, terminology and study questions in most of our seminars. It is available in hard copy from The School Locker , as an e-book from the publisher, Wiley , and on-line from the library. If you are completing the HR field of study, I strongly recommend you purchase this text book to give you a foundation to refer back to as you complete other specialist and more advanced courses.

Practitioner Resources

The field of Strategic HRM is extremely dynamic and most books date quickly. It is therefore important to read, think, and explore further than the prescribed readings. Students are expected to be "current" on how the issues in this course are "played-out" in actual organisations. Therefore, students are expected to be familiar with current HRM news. An easy way to get updated is to routinely check key SHRM Professional Organisations' Websites:

The Australian Human Resource Institute: www.ahri.com.au

The Society for Human Resource Management: www.shrm.org

The Society for Industrial and Organisational Psychology: www.siop.org

Academy of Human Resource Development: www.ahrd.org

Association for Talent & Development: www.td.org

International Society for Performance Improvement: www.ispi.org

Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development: www.cipd.co.uk

Learn (Blackboard)

All courses are supported by the UQ Learning Management System, Blackboard Learn, which provides an interactive environment for the students and teachers. This is accessible via PC or Mac or Mobile (Apps) environment.

To log on to Blackboard, go to https://learn.uq.edu.au.

As Blackboard is a web‐based application, you will need a recently‐released browser for your computer. For the latest Release Notes for Bb Student, see http://en-gb.help.blackboard.com/Bb_Student/z_Release_Notes_Bb_Student .

For help with Learn.UQ (Blackboard), see https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/learnuq-blackboard-help/learnuq-blackboard-contacts or contact AskUs https://web.library.uq.edu.au/contact-us or telephone 334 64312.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Course overview & preparations (Seminar): (Note that "Date" refers to the week beginning Monday. Please attend class on the day scheduled as per your enrolment.) Topics: Introductions, Learning approach, Assessments. Introducing Strategic HRM and the role of Org Design and Staffing. Definitions, debates and ethical perspectives. Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 1); DiStefano; Deloitte 2019 (pg 1-10)

Week 2: Analysing & Designing Jobs (Seminar): Employment arrangements - traditional and emerging, Job analysis, developing position descriptions, job design, including job crafting Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 5); Berg 2008; Deloitte 2018 (pg 73-78); Parker; Erez

Week 3: Recruitment in Practice (Seminar): Strategic recruitment, labour markets, recruitment strategies, employer branding, traditional recruitment methods, roles in recruitment. Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 6); Banerjee; Breagh; Jepsen 2015; Ployhart; Lievens

Week 4: Recruitment Techniques & Trends (Seminar): Legal, ethical and diversity issues, contemporary and innovative recruitment methods, developing recruitment ads Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 6); McKins 2015; Frasca; Nikolaou; Winstanley; Deloitte 2019 (pg 71-76)

Week 5: Selection in Practice (Seminar): Strategic selection, selection policies, selection decision making, selection criteria, candidate fit, traditional selection methods, validity & reliability, roles in selection. Poster Presentations: Weeks 2, 3 and 4. Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 7); Bolander; Ployhart; Tippins; Barrick

Week 6: Selection Techniques & Trends (Seminar): Legal, ethical and diversity issues, contemporary selection methods, designing selection processes, evaluating recruitment and selection. Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 7); Hoek; Mondore; Liu; Van Esch; Laumer; Langer; Winsborough; Raub
Week 7: Specialist Staffing Campaigns (Seminar): Executive recruitment, assessment centres. Presentation of R&S strategies. Readings/Ref: Cappelli Hamori; Hoffman; Rupp; Ren; Kleinmann

Week 8: Joining the Organisation (Seminar): Employment offers, employment conditions, induction processes targeted for employee needs. Poster Presentations: Weeks 5, 6 & 7. Readings/Ref: Bauer; Stone (Ch 9.6); Milligan; Cable

Week 9: Talent Management (Seminar): Defining Talent Management, alternate perspectives, ethical & practical issues, talent management processes. Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 10); Cappelli Keller; Freedman; Kaiser (See other articles in this issue for further evidence.); Lynton; Swailes; Dai; Harsch

Week 10: HR Planning (Seminar): HR planning and HR strategy, environmental influences, approaches to HR planning, casualisation, globalisation, the gig economy. Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 2); McKins 2015; Peterson; Winstanley

Week 11: Organisational Design (Seminar): Defining organisational design, traditional and contemporary models, design choices and evaluating fit to business strategy. Poster Presentations: Weeks 8, 9 & 10. Readings/Ref: Dunbar; Nadler Ch 3; Jordan; McKinsey 2017

Week 12: Organisational restructuring (Seminar): Triggers for organisational re-design, the re-design process, implementing restructures, managing stakeholders through change Readings/Ref: Stone (Ch 15); Guo; Klehe; Snell; Nadler Ch 9; Luo; Campion

Week 13: Course review and case analysis (Seminar): Entrepreneurial growth, review of key concepts and practices, case analysis preparation. Personal learning reflection. Poster Presentation: Weeks 11 & 12. Readings/Ref: DiStefano; Raisch; Deloitte 2019

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Tutorial - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Tutorial (AIT) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. All students are strongly encouraged to complete the module. Some course coordinators may require you to complete the module as part of your assessment.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

In accord with student learning as a joint responsibility of student and instructor along with the Professional Code of Conduct (Section 1.2), students are expected to immediately inform the lecturer if they have any course-related concerns in order to provide adequate time for the Course Coordinator to make any necessary adjustments to ensure that learning outcomes are being achieved throughout the semester. A formal teaching and course evaluation will be conducted later in the semester.

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

School of Business Assessment Guidelines

Student Notices are posted progressively on the UQ Business School website http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student_notices

Reviewing Assessment

The Assessment Viewing Register procedures are:

Go to Current Student – Student Notices – Exam Viewing – online form here then log in.

Students should log in using their normal UQ name (e.g. s9990000) and password.

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment

Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment are not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers.

The sessions are scheduled during weeks 1 and 2 of the teaching week in both semester one and two; and in the case of summer examinations during weeks 1 and 2 in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands.

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation.

Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results

Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the Course Coordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the Course Coordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Associate Dean Academic of the BEL Faculty.

Re-marks

A student who believes the judgement of their work and the resulting mark or grade they were awarded for a piece of assessment does not reflect his/her performance as measured against the published assessment criteria, can apply for a re-mark.

The result of re-marking should not unfairly disadvantage other students.

A re-mark will be conducted once only on the piece of assessment.

In all cases, a re-mark will replace the original mark for the piece of assessment in the calculation of the final grade which could result in a higher or lower grade.

Grounds for consideration

An application for a re-mark is considered only when the student:

has sought and received feedback about their performance on the assessment from the course coordinator or lecturer concerned (or has viewed the piece of assessment and, where available, model answers/written comments or other feedback/etc); and

after receiving feedback, provides a sound written academic case to demonstrate how the mark or grade awarded for an entire task, or a discrete component of a multi-component task, does not reflect their performance against the advertised criteria and standards for that work. Students should reference the published assessment criteria for the piece of assessment and clearly show where they believe there are grounds for reconsideration of the assessment judgement.

The desire for extra marks to achieve a higher final course grade is not sufficient grounds for a re-mark.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

Computing Facilities

Courses offered by UQ Business School utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskIT, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia.

Professional Code of Conduct

The University of Queensland has strict guidelines around academic integrity, plagiarism, and assessment which should be carefully read in the policy sections of this eCP. In addition, UQ Business School expects that as future business leaders and entrepreneurs, our students will adhere to the highest standards of ethical behaviour in all of their coursework.

Attendance at lectures, tutorials, seminars and workshops is expected and students are reminded that lecture recordings are not designed to replace in-class interactions and learning experiences.

It is a common courtesy to your fellow students and teaching staff to ensure you are on time for all class sessions, that you come prepared and are ready and willing to actively participate in class activities. Leaving class early is also disruptive to your fellow students and lecturers/tutors.

Please use professional etiquette and courtesy in all of your written and oral communication with teaching staff and fellow students. Please also ensure that all written correspondence includes your name and student ID.

Another matter of etiquette is the use of phones, laptops and other electronic devices in class. Some in-class activities may require the use of devices but otherwise we expect that these will be switched off during class time.

Students are not to make their own recordings of lectures or tutorials as this contravenes the UQ policy on Student Integrity and Misconduct. This includes taking photos of lecture or tutorial slides and/or other materials. Teaching staff will distribute all required materials to you via Blackboard.

Course Coordinators and lecturers may set additional requirements and expectations for their respective courses.

Other Course Guidelines

Professional Cod e of Conduct: This goes without saying, but we’ll say it anyway

Your success in this course derives from the same kinds of behaviours and attitudes that will make you successful in your careers. As business leaders and entrepreneurs, you will be expected to adhere to high standards of ethical behaviour and hard work. The work habits you develop and demonstrate in this course will make an important contribution to your future success. What constitutes a proper code of conduct includes: coming on time to all class sessions, coming prepared, actively participating at all class sessions, respecting your workgroup and other class participants, accepting responsibility for your own learning, contributing your fair share to group assignments, participating at group meetings, meeting all due dates, and following rules on plagiarism.

Please respect all students’ and lecturers’ time by doing your best at all times. This means, for instance, coming prepared for class. The focus will not be on finding the “right answer” to a given problem, but rather on a discussion and an “it depends” attitude. This demands that you are prepared and put an effort into each class.

If the problem of free-riders occurs, please deal with this issue immediately with your group member. To excel as business leaders and entrepreneurs, you will need to deal openly, constructively, and in a timely manner with any issues in your team. Excuses, made afterwards, due to this reason will not be accepted.

​Cheating, plagiarism or fabrication of other peoples’ material will not under any circumstances be tolerated. In order to eliminate cheating at UQ we adopt the Turnitin system on Blackboard, which cross-checks your submissions with literature and other texts. Any assignments where incorrect quotes are found are then reported to the course examiner.

Please discuss any comments on the course as soon as possible with the course coordinator so that we may be able to take them into account as soon as possible. The responsibility for a good course is a mutual commitment and therefore also lies in your hands.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Welcome to MGTS7612, The Fundamentals of Designing and Staffing Organisations.

This course addresses the different ways an organisation works with people, through a range of employment arrangements, to execute its aims or business objectives. The course takes a holistic perspective on staffing the organisation, including the design of organisation structures and takeing a talent pipeline approach to examine how business needs are matched to individual skills and experience. The latest research in business ethics and positive and strengths-based approaches to HRM underpins the course.

Students will learn about organisation design, workforce planning, the job analysis process, identifying key selection criteria, employment types and options (e.g., including contracting and outsourcing), developing position descriptions, identifying an employment value proposition to attract the right talent, writing job ads, short-listing processes, selection processes and induction. We will also consider the range of circumstances where staffing processes are needed, such as business expansion, responding to strategic change and restructuring. Legal and ethical considerations (e.g., equity, diversity, wellbeing) will be addressed for each area. A major focus will be equipping you to apply academic evidence to your practice as an HR professional or as a line manager.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Recent student feedback has confirmed that the group activities included in Semester 1, 2019 have been helpful and engaging. The textbook and on-line learning material also introduced recently have been well utilised. No major changes are planned for this semester. Your ongoing feedback during the semester will be welcomed to inform any future changes.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

other_learning_activities_information:
Learning is a dynamic process, and development of evidence-based expertise requires adapting the content and timing of learning activities to learner characteristics. Thus, you should note that the seminar program outlined is only a guide to the material to be covered in the course and not a rigid schedule for when and what material will be covered. Any changes to the timing and focus on specific material will be mutually decided in class based on student learning needs.

Seminar Approach

Our weekly seminar sessions will be content rich but also interactive. Each week there will be material that I will present, building on the weekly readings. You will get much more value from these if you have read the assigned papers. Each seminar will include brief interactive activities and usually a case study analysis. We will use a number of web based tools to facilitate gathering your input during class and I will encourage you to use the Blackboard discussion tool between classes for questions and to further develop your thinking. I will encourage you to reflect on your learning regularly and to relate new learning to your prior experience on a week by week basis. I have designed the assessments to give you feedback on your learning along the way, and I will also be regularly asking you to give me feedback on how the learning process is working for you.

Student Responsibility

In accord with student learning as a joint responsibility of student and instructor along with the Professional Code of Conduct and Professional Communication Standards (see below), please let me know immediately if you have any major course-related concerns so that I can make any necessary adjustments to ensure that learning outcomes are being achieved throughout the semester.

It is also your responsibility to attend classes and consult designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

Professional Code of Conduct

Your success in this course derives from the same kinds of behaviours and attitudes that will make you successful in your careers. As business leaders and entrepreneurs, you will be expected to adhere to high standards of ethical behaviour and hard work. The work habits you develop and demonstrate in this course will make an important contribution to your future success. What constitutes a proper code of conduct includes: coming on time to all class sessions, coming prepared, actively participating at all class sessions, respecting your workgroup and other class participants, accepting responsibility for your own learning, contributing your fair share to group assignments, participating at group meetings, meeting all due dates, following rules on plagiarism, and respecting the role and decisions of the teachers.

Please respect all students’ and lecturers’ time by doing your best at all times. This means, for instance, coming on time and prepared for class. The focus of the course will not be on finding the “right answer” to a given problem, but rather on a discussion and an “it depends” attitude. This demands that you assume responsibility for your actions, meet deadlines, graciously accept written and oral feedback, and demonstrate respect of self and others.

If the problem of free-riders occurs, please deal with this issue immediately with your group member. To excel as business leaders and entrepreneurs, you will need to deal openly, constructively, and in a timely manner with any issues in your team. Therefore, excuses made afterwards will not be accepted.

​Cheating, plagiarism or fabrication of other people's material will not be tolerated under any circumstances. In order to eliminate cheating at UQ we adopt the Turnitin system on Blackboard, which cross-checks your submissions with literature and other texts. Any assignments where incorrect quotes are found are then reported to the course examiner.

Please discuss any comments on the course as soon as possible with the course coordinator so that we may be able to take them into account as soon as possible. The responsibility for a good course is a mutual commitment and therefore also lies in your hands.

Professional Communication Standards

Professionalism requires professional communication skills and professional dress. All assignments and class discussions must be presented in a professional manner. Attention to using non-sexist and non-stigmatising language is essential. Intercultural communication sensitivity is expected at all times. All assignments must be carefully copy-edited prior to submission.

Administration

All content related quetions and specific questions regarding the administration of this course should be directed to me as the course coordinator. General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to BEL Student Centre: Level 2, Colin Clark Building #39, St Lucia Campus.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

-

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

-

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

-

Other Requirements & Comments:

Grades will be allocated according to University-wide standards of criterion Based Assessment.

late_submission:
Students must fill in an Application for Extension of Assessment Due Date and provide supporting documentation to Assessment, Examinations & Misconducts Coordinator https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-extension at least 24 hours prior to the submission date.

Your application must be submitted at the location and by the due date specified in Section 5.3 of the relevant Electronic Course Profile (ECP).

Supporting documentation such as a medical certificate, funeral notice etc. must be provided. Scanned or photographed copies should be attached to your email.

For an application on medical grounds, the medical practitioner must not be a near relative or close associate. Examples of near relatives are partner, child, brother, sister, parent. Examples of close associates are close friends, neighbours and partners or children of colleagues.

Extension criteria are applied consistently for equity reasons.

You may discuss your situation with your course coordinator, but you still need to make a formal extension request using this form.

Applications on medical grounds will be approved for the number of calendar days the medical certificate indicates you were unfit for study. You are expected to act in a timely manner and must make an appointment as soon as your condition impacts on your ability to study.

If you have a continuing condition you should contact Student Services to arrange a Student Access Plan(Disability) [SAPD]. You must still submit the application form

Non-permissible circumstances

Extensions will not be granted where the School is not satisfied you took reasonable measures to avoid the circumstances that contributed to you not submitting by the due date. The following are not grounds for an extension:

holiday arrangements (including overseas travel);

misreading a due date;

social and leisure events;

moving house;

pressure of work/competing deadlines;

computer issues.

The due dates for assignments must be adhered to and you are reminded to keep a copy of your work, in case of lost assignments or disputes over grading.

You will incur penalties if your work is submitted late (i.e. after the due date and without an approved extension).

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue. The penalty once calculated is deducted from the marks awarded for the assessment. Assessment submitted more than 10 days after the due date will receive zero marks.
other_assessment_information:
ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise advised by your course coordinator, all written assignments are to be electronically submitted using Blackboard (Learn) https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . The instructions for submission below can also be found on the "Assessment" link in Blackboard. The online submission is in addition to any other submission requirements that appear in this eCP.

IMPORTANT: Online Submission

Ensure you receive a Turnitin submission ID or Learn.UQ confirmation number, and retain this number as proof of submission. If you do not receive proof of submission, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful and take steps to address the issue. It is your responsibility to ensure the assignment has been successfully submitted. Any requests to waive the late submission penalty without proof of submission will not be considered under any circumstances.

The method of submission will be decided by your Course Coordinator, either Turnitin or Blackboard. Upload your assignment using the relevant link created for your use .

INSTRUCTIONS FOR SUBMITTING YOUR ASSIGNMENT & CONFIRMING YOUR SUBMISSION

TURNITIN

Submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/0#0

Upload your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/1#1

Review and submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/2#2

Confirm your submission - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1921/3#3

BLACKBOARD

Access your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/learnuq-blackboard-help/learnuq-assessment/blackboard-assignments/assignment-submission-instructions

Submit your assignment - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/1#1

Add comments - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/2#2

Submit - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/3#3

Review/resubmit - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/node/1912/4#4

For ALL, individual and Team assessment items:

The assignment must be submitted electronically using the online submission link located in the Blackboard Assessment menu https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . Hardcopy submissions are not required except for items that are too difficult to upload through the online submission links eg posters and multi-modal assessment tasks.

FOR TEAM ASSIGNMENTS

All team assignments must have ONE nominated representative's details at the top of the coversheet, with details of other team members listed on the lower section.

Students should keep a list of their team members' full names and contact details.

Please check with your course coordinator if online submission is required for a team assignment.

REFERENCING AND CITING

Assignments must be substantially your own work. If you wish to report another author's point of view you should do so in your own words, and properly footnote the reference in accordance with the school style. Direct quotations should be used sparingly, form a small part of your work, and must be placed in quotation marks and referenced.

Any material taken from texts and other references, including electronic resources, CD-ROMS, and the Internet, must be acknowledged using the accepted School style.

Students are encouraged to discuss issues that arise in this course together, however, the written work you submit must be entirely your own . Similarly, you must not help another student to cheat by lending assignments (present or past).

For more information on referencing styles, visit the library or see: https://web.library.uq.edu.au/research-tools-techniques/referencing/referencing-style-guides .

If you do not reference correctly, you could be found guilty of academic misconduct. Please see this link for more information: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/student-integrity-and-conduct/academic-integrity-and-student-conduct.

For more information on assessment, please review the UQ Assessment Policy at https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment

DEFERRED EXAMS

Midsemester Examinations

A student who fails to sit the midsemester examination and/or the quizzes at the scheduled times will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the School receives a deferred request on Si-net with appropriate documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence. The application must be submitted either prior to the scheduled date of the midsemester exam, or no later than five days after the midsemester examination. All deferred midsemester examinations within UQ Business School will take place on Saturday 12 October 2019 . A timetable for the deferred midsemester examinations will be available early in the week commencing 8 October 2019 .

This will be the only opportunity to take the deferred midsemester examination. Any student who is granted a deferred midsemester examination and who fails to attend at the appointed time and place will be awarded a mark of zero as per the PPL rules. There is no opportunity to defer a deferred examination under any circumstances.

The process for applying for deferred midsemester exams is as follows:

Applications for deferred midsemester examinations must be made online through Si-Net. Information about the process of applying and the online form can be found via mySi-net > myRequests > Deferred Examination Requests .

You will receive an email from Si-net to your student email account indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

Be aware of Discretionary exam request – if you use the discretionary deferred option, it will NOT be available to you again.

If you are applying on medical grounds or other eligible circumstances DO NOT use the Discretionary option.

The timetable will be found on the UQ Business School webpage > Student > Student Notices > under Deferred Midsemester Examination (see http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student-notices )

Students who have three or more deferred exams will be notified by the Examinations Section to put in an Alternative Arrangements form.

Students who have two deferred exams will have a choice of when to sit both exams.

Any enquiries regarding midsemester deferred examinations should be directed to the Assessment, Examinations and Misconducts Coordinator, exams.officer@business.uq.edu.au .

Saturday midsemester examination dates:

Saturday 31 August 2019

Saturday 7 September 2019

Saturday 14 September 2019

Final Examinations

A student who is unable to sit a scheduled final examination must apply for a deferred examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held.

The process for applying for deferred final exams is as follows:

Applications for deferred midsemester examinations must be made online through Si-Net. Information about the process of applying and the online form can be found via mySi-net > myRequests > Deferred Examination Requests .

You will receive an email from Si-net to your student email account indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

The Examination Section and Faculties may approve applications for deferred examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Deferred examinations are not automatically granted. For further information (such as evidence required and time limits) please see My.UQ https://my.uq.edu.au/ .

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

Supplementary assessment is an additional opportunity to demonstrate that the learning requirements for an eligible course have been satisfied and that the graduate attributes for the course have been attained. Supplementary assessment may only be granted where PPL 3.10.09 Supplementary Assessment – procedures allow.

A grade of 4 (or P) is the highest grade that can be awarded in a course where supplementary assessment has been granted. For further information on supplementary assessment please see my.UQ https://my.uq.edu.au/ . If you receive a grade of 3 for this course, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment depending on the reason that you have failed.

For all UQ Business School courses supplementary assessment is weighted at 100% and covers the learning outcomes for the course. This means that the marks obtained for any progressive assessment throughout the semester are disregarded and the final result for the course is based entirely on the performance in the supplementary assessment.
